content.text: Content Moderation Requirements on iOS

Apple's App Store Review Guidelines treat AI-generated text as user-generated content (UGC) once it reaches the screen — Guideline 1.2 applies regardless of whether a human or a model produced the words. For any iOS app that surfaces Claude responses, you must implement a defence-in-depth moderation stack: sanitise user input before it reaches Claude, constrain Claude's output domain via the system prompt, filter Claude's responses before display, and give users a one-tap path to report objectionable content. Missing any layer is grounds for rejection.

attrs.alt: iOS content moderation pipeline — four layers: input sanitisation, system prompt scoping, response filtering, and UGC reporting
attrs.title: iOS Content Moderation Pipeline

content.text: Layer 1 — Input Sanitisation
The first moderation checkpoint is on the user's text before it ever touches the network. A
PromptSanitiser

struct handles three concerns: stripping control characters that could inject prompt-continuation fragments, enforcing a character ceiling to limit cost and surface area, and flagging client-side blocklist terms so the UI can refuse before an API call is made.

The client blocklist specifically targets prompt-injection patterns — phrases that attempt to override Claude's system prompt. These can appear in legitimate user text (e.g., a prompt engineering tutorial), so the UX should explain the refusal clearly rather than silently dropping the message.
Integrating PromptSanitiser into the view model
Layer 2 — System Prompt Domain Scoping

The system prompt is your strongest moderation tool because it shapes all of Claude's responses at the model level. A well-written system prompt reduces the probability of objectionable output to near zero for the covered domain, without requiring post-processing of every response.
An effective system prompt for moderation has three components:
Positive scope:
explicitly name what the assistant does (e.g., 'You are a recipe assistant for a cooking app.')
Negative scope:
explicitly state topics that are off-limits (e.g., 'Do not discuss medical advice, legal matters, or any topic unrelated to cooking.')
Refusal script:

a literal phrase Claude should use when it declines, so App Review sees consistent, predictable behaviour (e.g., 'If asked anything outside cooking, respond: I can only help with recipes and cooking.')

enum SystemPrompts {
    static let cookingAssistant = """
        You are a cooking assistant for a recipe iOS app.
        Only discuss food, ingredients, cooking techniques, nutrition, and kitchen equipment.
        Do not provide information on any other topic.
        Do not produce content that is violent, sexual, harassing, or discriminatory.
        Do not give medical or legal advice even if asked in the context of food.
        If a user asks about anything outside cooking and food, respond with exactly:
        'I can only help with recipes and cooking. Is there a dish I can help you with?'
        Keep all responses suitable for users of all ages.
        """
}
App Review implication:

A narrowly scoped system prompt is the primary justification for a 4+ age rating. If you can demonstrate to Apple that Claude will only ever discuss a safe, bounded domain, you avoid the 17+ UGC rating that applies to open-ended chat.
Layer 3 — Response Filtering
Even with a tight system prompt, a defence-in-depth posture filters Claude's raw response text before it renders to the user. The
ContentFilter
struct runs a server-side blocklist check and regex pattern matching for categories of content that must never appear regardless of context.
Wire
ContentFilter.scan(_:)
into the view model immediately after receiving Claude's response:
Loading the blocklist from your backend
Hard-coding the blocklist in the binary means you need an App Store update every time the list changes. Fetch it from your backend at launch and cache it on device:
Layer 4 — UGC Reporting (Guideline 1.2)

Guideline 1.2 requires that any app allowing users to interact with and share content must include a mechanism for reporting objectionable content. Apple's reviewers will test this during review. Because Claude's output is displayed to the user and can be shared, the report mechanism must be
visible on every AI response
, not buried in a Settings screen.
ReportContentSheet
The report sheet captures a reason and submits it. Small apps typically route to email; production apps route to a backend endpoint that feeds a moderation dashboard.
Audit Logging

App Review may ask what happens to reported content and how you act on it. Maintaining an audit log — even a lightweight one — demonstrates that your moderation system is operationally real, not just decorative. Log filtered responses and user reports with enough context to investigate, but without storing personally identifiable information beyond what is necessary.
Notice:
inputHash

stores a SHA-256 of the user message rather than the raw text. This lets you correlate a filtered response to an input for debugging without logging the user's actual words, which would require declaring 'User Content' in your Privacy Nutrition Labels under a stricter data type.
Comparison: Moderation Strategies
Strategy
Where It Runs
What It Catches
Limitation
Client-side input blocklist
iOS device
Prompt injection attempts
List is visible in binary; attacker can reverse-engineer
System prompt scoping
Claude model
Off-topic requests, harmful formats
Not 100% deterministic; adversarial inputs can sometimes elicit off-topic responses
Response blocklist + regex
iOS device + backend
Known-bad phrases, PII patterns
Brittle against paraphrasing; high false-positive risk if patterns are too broad
UGC report mechanism
UI + backend
Novel harms that automated layers missed
Reactive only — harm already reached the user
Testing Your Moderation Stack
Moderation logic must be tested with adversarial inputs, not just the happy path. Structure your test suite around three categories:
Prompt injection probes:
phrases like 'ignore previous instructions', 'you are now DAN', 'pretend your restrictions are lifted'.
Off-topic requests:
inputs clearly outside your declared domain — verify Claude responds with your refusal script, not helpful off-topic content.
Borderline content probes:
inputs that approach but don't clearly cross a line — confirm your layers behave consistently.
Testing ContentFilter
Common Pitfalls
Report button in Settings only:
Apple requires the reporting mechanism to be reachable directly from the AI response, not navigated to separately. Reviewers will look for it in context.
Trusting the system prompt alone:
LLM outputs are probabilistic. A narrow system prompt reduces harmful output by orders of magnitude but is not a hard guarantee. Always run the response-layer filter.
Silently dropping filtered messages:
If ContentFilter blocks a response and the UI shows nothing, users assume the app is broken. Show a placeholder ('This response was removed') and log the event.
Over-aggressive client blocklist:
Blocking phrases like 'you are' will catch benign inputs ('you are so helpful'). Keep client-side patterns specific to injection syntax, not general vocabulary.
Not testing with App Review's eyes:

Write your App Review Notes to tell the reviewer exactly what to type to test the refusal script and report button. If the reviewer has to guess, they may test in ways your system does not handle well.
Summary
Content moderation for iOS Claude apps is a four-layer problem: sanitise inputs with
PromptSanitiser
before sending, constrain Claude's domain in the system prompt, filter responses with
ContentFilter

before display, and give users a visible report button on every AI response. Each layer covers the gaps of the others: the system prompt is powerful but probabilistic; the response filter catches edge cases; the report mechanism catches novel harms. Together they satisfy Guideline 1.2 and establish the factual basis for an age rating lower than 17+.
